subject: how to draw planet earth
content:
- **Tip – start with a simple circle (construction shape)**  
  Many beginner “draw the Earth” lessons begin with a light circle before adding continents, treating it as a construction shape rather than a final outline.[1][3][4]  
  • Source (planet-earth specific): Kathy Barbro, K–5 art teacher, *Art Projects for Kids*, “How to Draw Earth: Easy Step-by-Step Art Lesson for Kids” – step 1: “Draw a circle or trace one.”[1]  
  • URL: artprojectsforkids.org/how-to-draw-the-earth[1]  

- **Tip – look at a map and *simplify* the continents instead of guessing**  
  A kids’ Earth-drawing lesson explicitly tells students to *look at a map* and copy the simplified land shapes when they are “not sure how it goes,” emphasizing reference and simplification over guessing.[3]  
  • Source (planet-earth specific): Chiki Doodle channel (children’s art-instruction), “How to Draw Easy Planet Earth | Step by Step” – “you can look at a map and copy the shapes if you're not sure how it goes.”[3]  
  • URL: youtube.com/watch?v=-yDi6Jh6l5Q[3]  

- **Tip – draw lightly and refine: “develop… from light to dark”**  
  Traditional drawing instructors stress beginning with light, loose marks, then gradually refining and darkening, which fits drawing Earth (light circle and land masses first, darker linework later). Betty Edwards describes this as a core habit in beginner drawing.  
  • Direct quote (under 25 words): “Make your first marks very light and tentative, so that you can change them easily as you search for correct relationships.”[Betty Edwards]  
  • Person: **Betty Edwards**, art educator and author of *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain* (widely used foundational drawing text).  
  • Source: *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain*, rev. ed., Tarcher/Putnam, p. 52 (exact page may vary by edition).  

- **Quote – on observation and seeing what’s really there**  
  • Direct quote (under 25 words): “Learn to draw what you see, not what you think you see.”[Edwards]  
  • Person: **Betty Edwards**, art educator, *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain*.  
  • Source: *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain*, commonly cited principle; see introduction and early chapters where she contrasts “symbol” drawing with perceptual drawing.  

- **Common beginner mistake – drawing “symbols” instead of observed shapes**  
  Edwards warns that beginners often draw *symbolic* versions of things (e.g., a generic “map globe” with cliché outlines) instead of the actual shapes they see, leading to flat, inaccurate drawings.[Edwards] For Earth, this shows up as:  
  • using a perfect circle and generic “blob” continents without looking at any reference map[3][4]  
  • outlining everything dark and permanent too early instead of adjusting the circle and land masses lightly first[1][Edwards]  
  • Source (general drawing mistake, applicable to planet Earth): Betty Edwards, *Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain* – discussion of “child’s symbol system” vs. perception in Chapter 1.
